nativeRange.habitat: Rocky shorelines interspersed with Vallisneria beds along the north-western coast of Lake Malawi, generally between Cape Kaiser and Lundo and from Chirmbo Point to Charo.
nativeRange.biotope: Lake Malawi mbuna rocky shore (rift-lake hardwater)

decorPreferences: Stacked rockwork forming caves and territories
Open swimming lane along the front of the tank
Fine sand or smooth gravel substrate
Hardy plants only — Vallisneria, Anubias or Java fern attached to hardscape (most plants will be dug up or grazed)

diet.notes: Generally regarded as an opportunistic micropredator in the wild, taking small invertebrates from rockwork and Vallisneria, but also grazes algae. In aquaria a varied diet built around a quality cichlid pellet and spirulina-based flake works well, supplemented with frozen brine shrimp, mysis or daphnia. Avoid heavy red-meat foods (beef heart, tubifex) which have been linked to Malawi bloat in mbuna.

goodWith: Other moderately peaceful Lake Malawi mbuna (e.g. Rusty Cichlid, Cynotilapia, similarly sized Pseudotropheus)
Synodontis catfish (e.g. Synodontis lucipinnis, S. petricola)
Larger plecos that tolerate hard alkaline water
avoidWith: Soft-water community fish (tetras, rasboras, most South American cichlids)
Highly aggressive mbuna (Melanochromis auratus, M. chipokae)
Other yellow or similarly coloured Labidochromis that may hybridise (e.g. L. sp. 'Hongi', L. sp. 'Perlmutt')
Slow-moving or long-finned fish (angelfish, fancy goldfish, bettas)
Dwarf shrimp and small snails (likely to be eaten)

summary: The Yellow Lab Cichlid (Labidochromis caeruleus) is a lemon-yellow Lake Malawi mbuna widely considered among the gentlest of its group, making it a popular first rift-lake cichlid — though it still needs hard, alkaline water and a rock-built tank.
careNotes: Labidochromis caeruleus is widely sold under the trade names 'Electric Yellow', 'Electric Yellow Lab' and 'Lemon Yellow Lab'. It is generally considered the gentlest of the commonly kept mbuna and is often recommended as a beginner's first Malawi cichlid, but it is still a territorial rift-lake fish that benefits from a tank of at least 200 L with a long footprint (at least 90 cm) and abundant rockwork forming caves and visual breaks. Hard alkaline water (pH 7.8-8.6, gH 10-20, kH 6-12, around 24-26°C) most closely matches its Lake Malawi habitat between Cape Kaiser and Lundo. Aggression is generally low for an mbuna but climbs sharply with multiple males in one tank; a harem of one male to three or more females is the usual recommendation, with groups of four or more helping to spread aggression. Plant choices are limited — most softer plants will be dug up or grazed, while Vallisneria and Anubias or Java fern attached directly to rocks are usually tolerated.
breedingNotes: Maternal mouthbrooder. Females typically carry 5-30 eggs in the buccal cavity for around 3-4 weeks before releasing free-swimming fry, and will often continue to shelter fry in the mouth for several days afterwards. Spawning is most reliably triggered in a harem (1 male : 3-4 females) in clean, warm (~26-27°C) hard alkaline water. Holding females are best stripped or moved to a separate tank to maximise fry yield, as fry are otherwise predated by tankmates once released.

primary.notes: Used as primary source because the slice primary Aquarium Co-Op has no dedicated care page for this species (search returns only stickers and unrelated blog posts).

additional.notes: Taxonomy (Cichlidae / Cichliformes), max length 8.1 cm SL, temperature 23-26°C cross-check.

additional.name: Aquarium Source
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (~10 cm), lifespan 6-10 yr, tank size 40-50 US gal (~150-190 L), pH 7.2-8.8, gH 10-20.

additional.notes: Cross-checked temperament/grouping (semi-aggressive, harem), tank ~114 L floor, temperature 23-29°C, mouthbrooding behaviour.

fish.breedingNotes: The Yellow Lab Cichlid is a maternal mouthbrooder that tends to spawn readily in harem groups kept in suitably hard, warm water.
